articles.excerpt: Artificial intelligence will not replace the architect. But it may finally ask us to become the kind of thinkers we have always claimed to be.
articles.body:
For decades, architecture has celebrated creativity while increasingly rewarding production. We have become highly proficient at generating drawings, coordinating consultants, satisfying regulations, and navigating procurement. Much of contemporary practice is measured not by the quality of thought, but by the volume and speed of deliverables.

Artificial intelligence has arrived at an uncomfortable moment for the profession because it excels at precisely those activities that have come to dominate architectural practice. Drafting, documentation, code lookups, specification writing, precedent searches, visualization, scheduling, and coordination are all becoming increasingly susceptible to automation.

The instinctive response has been anxiety. Will architects become obsolete?

The more interesting question is whether we have mistaken our tools for our profession.

Architecture has never been about drawings. Drawings are merely the language through which architectural decisions are communicated. Before there were CAD platforms, BIM models, or parametric software, there was design. Before there was design, there was judgment.

Judgment remains stubbornly resistant to automation.

Every meaningful architectural project begins with a problem that is only partially defined. The brief is incomplete. The client often does not know what they truly require. Budgets evolve. Regulations conflict. Cultural expectations remain unspoken. Environmental conditions introduce uncertainty. Human behaviour refuses to fit neatly within a spreadsheet.

These are not computational problems alone. They are problems of interpretation.

Artificial intelligence can propose thousands of alternatives. It cannot determine which question should have been asked in the first place.

This distinction matters.

The profession has long described architects as synthesizers—individuals capable of reconciling structure, climate, economics, regulations, culture, landscape, engineering, aesthetics, and human behaviour into coherent places. Yet much of daily practice leaves little room for synthesis. Time is consumed by production.

Ironically, AI may become the force that restores architecture to its intellectual foundations.

As repetitive tasks become automated, the value of the architect shifts from producing information to organising it; from drawing solutions to framing problems; from documenting decisions to making them.

This is a subtle but profound transition.

The architect of the coming decade may spend less time modelling a staircase and considerably more time determining whether the staircase belongs there at all.

The implications extend well beyond design software.

Architectural practice has traditionally operated as a sequence of disconnected phases: concept, schematic design, detailed design, tender documentation, construction, and handover. Information is repeatedly recreated, reformatted, and reinterpreted as projects move between consultants, contractors, authorities, and clients.

Artificial intelligence has the potential to reduce this fragmentation by maintaining continuity of knowledge throughout the project lifecycle. A decision made during concept design can inform specifications, quantities, procurement strategies, commissioning, facility management, and even future renovations.

In such a workflow, architecture begins to resemble systems engineering more than document production.

This shift requires a corresponding change in education.

Architectural schools have historically emphasised form-making, representation, and individual expression. These remain essential, but they are no longer sufficient. Tomorrow’s architects must become equally comfortable with systems thinking, data literacy, computational reasoning, environmental modelling, economics, behavioural science, and the ethics of automation.

The profession should resist the temptation to frame this as a competition between humans and machines.

It is not.

The more relevant comparison is between architects who effectively integrate artificial intelligence into their thinking and those who continue to regard it as merely another drafting tool.

History offers a useful perspective. The introduction of computer-aided drafting did not eliminate architects; it eliminated manual drafting as a competitive advantage. Building Information Modelling did not replace design; it redefined coordination. Artificial intelligence is unlikely to be different. It will not diminish the need for architectural judgment. It will raise expectations for its quality.

Clients will increasingly assume that routine tasks can be completed faster, more accurately, and at lower cost. What they will continue to seek—and perhaps value more highly—is clarity amid complexity.

That has always been the architect’s true responsibility.

The coming AI architect is therefore not defined by technical proficiency alone. Software will continue to evolve rapidly, and today’s tools will inevitably be replaced. Enduring value will come from the ability to ask better questions, construct better frameworks, recognise unintended consequences, and make decisions across multiple, often conflicting, objectives.

These are fundamentally human capabilities.

Artificial intelligence will become an extraordinary collaborator. It will analyse regulations, retrieve precedents, generate alternatives, identify inconsistencies, optimise layouts, and compress weeks of research into hours. It will almost certainly become indispensable to professional practice.

But it will not assume responsibility.

Responsibility—for safety, ethics, public value, cultural continuity, environmental stewardship, and the long-term consequences of design—remains inseparable from human judgment.

Perhaps that is AI’s greatest contribution to architecture.

Not that it will replace architects.

But that it will expose the difference between producing architecture and merely producing drawings.

## Further Reading

Alexander, C. (1964). *Notes on the Synthesis of Form*. Harvard University Press.

Cross, N. (2011). *Design Thinking: Understanding How Designers Think and Work*. Berg.

Lawson, B. (2006). *How Designers Think: The Design Process Demystified* (4th ed.). Architectural Press.

Schön, D. A. (1983). *The Reflective Practitioner: How Professionals Think in Action*. Basic Books.

Simon, H. A. (1996). *The Sciences of the Artificial* (3rd ed.). MIT Press.

Eastman, C., Teicholz, P., Sacks, R., & Liston, K. (2018). *BIM Handbook* (3rd ed.). Wiley.

Royal Institute of British Architects. (2025). *AI Report 2025*. RIBA.

McKinsey Global Institute. (2023). *The Economic Potential of Generative AI: The Next Productivity Frontier*.

World Economic Forum. (2025). *Future of Jobs Report 2025*.

ISO 19650 Series. *Organization and Digitization of Information about Buildings and Civil Engineering Works, Including Building Information Modelling (BIM).*
articles.title: The coming AI architect

articles.excerpt: The most resilient places are not perfectly planned. They are built to adapt.
articles.body:
Cities and software have more in common than they first appear.

Both are complex systems. Both serve millions of users with competing needs. Both must function continuously while evolving over time. And in both, the greatest failures rarely arise from poor engineering alone—they result from rigid thinking.

The technology industry accepted an uncomfortable truth many years ago: no amount of planning can predict how millions of people will actually use a product. The only reliable way to build something useful is to release, observe, learn, and improve.

Software is no longer shipped as a finished object. It is continuously refined.

Cities, however, are still too often planned as though perfection can be achieved on paper.

That assumption deserves to be challenged.

Urban plans frequently attempt to define how neighbourhoods should function decades into the future. Roads, public spaces, transport systems and land use are fixed through master plans that leave little room for experimentation. Yet cities are living systems. Demographics change. Economies shift. Climate introduces new constraints. Technologies alter mobility. People’s behaviour consistently defies prediction.

The most resilient cities are therefore not those that attempt to anticipate every future condition.

They are those that are designed to adapt.

Software offers an instructive model.

When a technology company develops a significant new feature, it rarely deploys it to every user on the first day. The feature is first released as a beta, often to a small group of users. Engineers observe how it performs under real conditions, identify unexpected behaviour, measure adoption, correct flaws and only then expand deployment.

Failure is expected.

Learning is designed into the process.

Urban planning could benefit from the same philosophy.

Imagine if a redesigned intersection were first implemented using temporary materials. If a cycle lane were tested for six months before becoming permanent. If a public square could evolve through observation rather than being completed in a single construction phase. If parking policies, street markets or traffic restrictions were introduced as experiments with clearly defined success criteria instead of permanent declarations.

Many cities around the world have begun adopting this approach through tactical urbanism, temporary public spaces and pilot mobility projects. The objective is not to avoid planning. It is to improve planning by allowing reality to participate in the design process.

Perhaps the most important lesson software offers is not technical but philosophical.

Successful software is remarkably democratic.

Applications may offer premium subscriptions, enterprise editions or additional features for paying customers. Yet the core experience must work reliably for everyone. Login must work. Navigation must work. Performance must work. Accessibility must work.

If the basic product fails for the majority of users, the software has failed—regardless of how sophisticated its premium features may be.

Cities should be judged by the same standard.

Urban infrastructure cannot become an ecosystem where excellence is reserved for isolated enclaves while the everyday experience of the majority remains neglected.

A luxury district with impeccable landscaping cannot compensate for neighbourhoods without functioning footpaths. An iconic convention centre does not offset unreliable public transport. Signature architecture cannot substitute for dependable drainage. A world-class airport says little about the quality of the street outside it.

The fundamentals must work for everyone.

Premium experiences have their place. Cities should contain exceptional parks, cultural institutions, business districts and high-end developments. But these are enhancements, not substitutes for universal functionality.

Just as software developers obsess over reducing friction for every user, cities should measure success through the ease with which ordinary citizens move, work, learn and interact with public space.

Good urbanism is not about creating impressive destinations.

It is about making ordinary days function well.

This requires a shift in how we define planning itself.

Planning should not be understood as producing certainty. It should be understood as designing systems capable of learning.

That means collecting data after projects are completed, measuring outcomes rather than intentions, modifying regulations when evidence demands it and accepting that iteration is not an admission of failure but a characteristic of intelligent systems.

Architecture has long embraced the idea that buildings evolve through use. Software has institutionalised continuous improvement. Cities should do the same.

The future of Indian urbanism will not depend solely on larger master plans or more ambitious infrastructure projects. It will depend equally on our willingness to test assumptions, measure outcomes and adapt policies in response to evidence.

The cities that thrive over the coming decades are unlikely to be those that were planned most comprehensively.

They will be those that remain most capable of learning.


## Further Reading

Alexander, C. (1965). *A City Is Not a Tree*. *Architectural Forum*.

Alexander, C. (1964). *Notes on the Synthesis of Form*. Harvard University Press.

Jacobs, J. (1961). *The Death and Life of Great American Cities*. Random House.

Brand, S. (1994). *How Buildings Learn: What Happens After They’re Built*. Viking.

Ries, E. (2011). *The Lean Startup*. Crown Business.

Mehaffy, M. W. (2017). *A City Is Not a Computer*. Sustasis Press.

Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, Government of India. *National Transit-Oriented Development Policy* (2017).

NITI Aayog. *India Urban Infrastructure Vision 2030* (2022).
articles.title: What Indian cities can learn from software

articles.excerpt: Beauty in landscape is not an applied finish. It is the visible expression of a place performing well.
articles.body:
Landscape architecture occupies a curious position in contemporary practice. It is almost universally acknowledged as essential, yet it is too often commissioned as the final layer of a project—an exercise in visual completion after the architecture has been resolved.

The consequence is familiar.

Rows of ornamental palms that offer no shade. Lawns sustained by excessive irrigation. Imported species that struggle against local climates. Sculptural planting that photographs well but contributes little to ecology or human comfort. Landscapes that satisfy a rendering before they satisfy a season.

They are attractive.

They are rarely beautiful.

There is an important distinction.

Beauty has never been merely a matter of appearance. In the Indian tradition, aesthetics has always been inseparable from experience. The *Nāṭyaśāstra* and the theory of *Rasa* do not describe beauty as an object but as something evoked through relationships, context and emotional resonance. A work succeeds not because every element is individually impressive, but because together they produce an experience that feels inevitable.

The medieval architectural treatise *Samarāṅgaṇa Sūtradhāra*, attributed to King Bhoja, similarly presents architecture not as an isolated object but as an integrated discipline in which proportion, orientation, water, vegetation, climate and human occupation form a coherent whole. The built environment was understood as a system rather than an assemblage of decorative components.

This distinction remains relevant.

A landscape should not exist to complete a building.

It should complete a place.

The difference is profound.

Too much of contemporary landscape architecture remains object-oriented. The discussion centres on paving patterns, feature walls, sculptures, imported trees and signature planting palettes. These elements are often treated as indicators of quality because they are immediately visible and easily photographed.

Yet none of them necessarily improves the environmental performance of the site.

A landscape that fails to reduce heat, capture stormwater, support biodiversity, improve soil health or encourage human occupation cannot be considered successful simply because it is visually pleasing.

Its beauty is superficial because its purpose is incomplete.

Frederick Law Olmsted understood this instinctively.

Although remembered for Central Park, Olmsted’s greatest contribution was not the creation of picturesque scenery but the recognition that landscape performs public work. Parks were conceived as infrastructure for physical health, mental restoration, social interaction and ecological resilience. Beauty emerged from these functions rather than existing independently of them.

Ian McHarg advanced this argument further in *Design with Nature*, insisting that landscape design should begin with ecological systems rather than stylistic preferences. Soil, hydrology, geology, vegetation and climate were not constraints to overcome but the very foundation of design.

More recently, practitioners such as Peter Walker, Kathryn Gustafson, James Corner and Kongjian Yu have demonstrated that contemporary landscapes can simultaneously manage water, restore ecosystems, create public life and produce memorable spatial experiences.

Their work is visually compelling precisely because it performs.

The most enduring landscapes rarely separate beauty from utility.

Indian cities offer countless reminders of this principle.

The historic stepwells of Gujarat are simultaneously hydraulic infrastructure, architecture, climate moderators and civic spaces. Temple tanks regulate water while creating places of gathering and ritual. Traditional orchards provided food, shade and biodiversity long before sustainability became professional vocabulary.

None of these landscapes were decorative.

Their beauty arose from necessity.

Modern development has often reversed this relationship.

Environmental systems are hidden beneath concrete while landscape is asked to compensate aesthetically. The site drains poorly, becomes excessively hot, consumes vast quantities of water and supports little ecological life, yet ornamental planting is expected to produce the appearance of environmental responsibility.

No amount of decorative planting can disguise a dysfunctional site.

Landscape architecture should instead begin with different questions.

How does rain move through this land?

Where can water be retained rather than discharged?

Which species already belong here?

How will this place feel in late May rather than in a rendered image?

Where will children instinctively gather?

Where will older people choose to sit?

Which trees will still define this place fifty years from now?

These questions are less glamorous than selecting exotic planting palettes, but they are infinitely more consequential.

A successful landscape is one that becomes increasingly valuable with time.

Its trees mature. Its ecology strengthens. Its soils improve. Its maintenance burden declines. Its public life becomes richer because people choose to occupy it rather than merely observe it.

The landscape ceases to be an object.

It becomes an ecosystem.

Perhaps this is where Indian aesthetic thought continues to offer an important lesson.

*Rasa* is not created by ornament alone. It emerges when every element contributes to a larger experiential whole. Likewise, landscape beauty cannot be reduced to colour, texture or form. These remain important artistic tools, but they acquire meaning only when they serve climate, ecology, movement, memory and human well-being.

Form, detached from purpose, is decoration.

Form rooted in environmental performance becomes beauty.

As climate change intensifies, water becomes scarcer and cities grow denser, this distinction will become increasingly difficult to ignore.

The future of landscape architecture will not be determined by how convincingly it imitates nature.

It will be determined by how intelligently it restores the relationship between people, ecology and place.

Only then does landscape cease to be the final aesthetic layer of a project.

It becomes the foundation upon which enduring places are built.

## Further Reading

Bhoja. *Samarāṅgaṇa Sūtradhāra*. (11th century). Various English translations and scholarly commentaries.

Bharata Muni. *Nāṭyaśāstra*. Various translations, including those by Adya Rangacharya and Manomohan Ghosh.

Olmsted, F. L. (1870). *Public Parks and the Enlargement of Towns*.

McHarg, I. L. (1969). *Design with Nature*. John Wiley & Sons.

Spirn, A. W. (1984). *The Granite Garden: Urban Nature and Human Design*. Basic Books.

Corner, J. (Ed.). (1999). *Recovering Landscape: Essays in Contemporary Landscape Architecture*. Princeton Architectural Press.

Waldheim, C. (Ed.). (2006). *The Landscape Urbanism Reader*. Princeton Architectural Press.

Yu, K. (2016). *The Art of Survival: Recovering Landscape Architecture*. Applied Landscape Ecology.

Thompson, I. H. (2000). *Ecology, Community and Delight: Sources of Values in Landscape Architecture*. Routledge.
articles.title: A case against decorative landscapes

articles.excerpt: Every drawing is a compact agreement about how the world will be made.
articles.body:
Architecture does not begin with information.

It begins with a conversation.

Not a conversation between architect and client, nor between architect and engineer, but between the architect and the design itself.

The first sketch asks a question.

The second attempts an answer.

The third challenges that answer.

Each drawing is less a record of certainty than an argument with the previous one. A wall moves. A courtyard expands. A stair rotates. A window disappears. Constraints emerge. New possibilities appear. The drawing speaks back—not literally, but through its consequences. Every line exposes another decision that must be made.

This is the true act of designing.

Drawing, in its earliest form, is not communication.

It is thinking.

The sketchbook is therefore not an archive of solutions but a record of evolving judgment. Architects do not draw because they already know the answer. They draw to discover what the answer might become.

At some point, however, something fundamental changes.

The conversation ends.

The drawing stops asking questions and begins giving instructions.

What was once a private dialogue between designer and design becomes a public agreement shared among clients, engineers, contractors, manufacturers, authorities and craftsmen. The same line that once explored an idea now determines where concrete is poured. A dimension that was tentative becomes contractual. A note that once served memory becomes an instruction carrying legal and financial consequences.

The drawing has changed its purpose.

It has become information.

Every construction drawing is a remarkably dense package of knowledge. It communicates geometry, material, sequence, tolerances, responsibility and intent simultaneously. It compresses thousands of individual decisions into a language that allows hundreds of people—many of whom will never meet—to build the same object.

A construction project is therefore not coordinated by meetings alone.

It is coordinated by shared information.

Seen this way, a building exists long before it is built.

It first exists as relationships between ideas. Then as sketches. Then as drawings. Then as specifications. Then as schedules. Then as contracts. Finally, months or years later, those layers of information acquire physical form through steel, concrete, brick and glass.

Matter follows information.

This transformation explains why errors in architecture are so often informational rather than physical. Concrete rarely fails because it does not know how to bear load. Buildings fail because conflicting information reaches the site. A superseded drawing, an omitted detail, an incorrect level or an ambiguous note can redirect months of work and millions in cost.

Construction is ultimately an exercise in synchronising information.

Building Information Modelling recognised this reality more clearly than its name suggests.

Its greatest contribution was not three-dimensional geometry but the recognition that drawings are no longer the primary repository of architectural knowledge. Instead, the drawing becomes one view into a much richer body of interconnected information. Geometry, materials, quantities, costs, performance requirements and maintenance data all describe the same building from different perspectives.

The building becomes a database before it becomes a building.

Artificial intelligence extends this evolution further.

Machines are beginning to understand relationships within architectural information. They can compare regulations against layouts, identify inconsistencies between specifications and drawings, predict clashes and retrieve decades of accumulated knowledge almost instantly.

Yet they cannot replace the earliest conversation.

They cannot experience the uncertainty that accompanies the first sketch or recognise the quiet moment when a designer realises that a project has finally become coherent.

That conversation remains deeply human.

Perhaps this is why architecture should not be understood primarily as the production of drawings.

It is the gradual transformation of thought into information.

The sketch is a conversation.

The drawing is an agreement.

The specification is a commitment.

The building is simply information made permanent.

Every line we draw participates in each of these stages. It begins by helping the architect understand the design. It ends by helping the world construct it.

The line itself never changes.

Its meaning does.
articles.title: Architecture is information

articles.excerpt: Emerging technologies are transforming how buildings are conceived and constructed, but they do not change the fundamental purpose of architecture. As our tools become more powerful, judgment, proportion, climate and human experience become more—not less—important.
articles.body:
*Technology changes what we can build. It does not change why we build.*

Every generation of architects believes it is living through an unprecedented technological revolution.

Today, the conversation centres around Mivan formwork, precast concrete, post-tensioned slabs, robotics, modular construction, artificial intelligence, digital fabrication and autonomous construction equipment. The promise is familiar: buildings delivered faster, more efficiently and with greater precision than ever before.

The technologies are undoubtedly transformative.

The conclusions drawn from them are sometimes less so.

History suggests that architecture has always evolved alongside construction technology. Roman concrete enabled the Pantheon. Structural steel gave rise to the skyscraper. Reinforced concrete liberated the modern movement from load-bearing walls. Elevators transformed urban density. Air-conditioning changed the geography of cities. Building Information Modelling redefined project coordination.

Each innovation expanded the architect’s vocabulary.

None rewrote the language itself.

This distinction matters.

Construction technology determines what is possible.

Architecture determines what is worthwhile.

Consider Mivan construction.

Its greatest contribution is not aluminium formwork. It is repetition. Buildings become more economical when layouts are standardised, structural grids are disciplined and floor plates remain consistent. Construction accelerates because variability is reduced.

This has profound consequences for design.

Architects accustomed to treating every floor as a unique composition must instead think in systems. Small decisions repeated hundreds of times become more important than dramatic gestures that occur only once. Efficiency becomes a design parameter rather than merely a construction objective.

Precast construction introduces different possibilities.

Walls, stairs, façade panels and structural components become manufactured products rather than site-built assemblies. Precision improves. Construction time shortens. Quality becomes more consistent because fabrication occurs under controlled conditions.

Yet precast also demands discipline.

Connections become more important than components.

Tolerance becomes a design issue.

Transportation begins influencing architecture long before aesthetics do.

Post-tensioning offers another example.

By reducing slab thickness and increasing structural spans, it creates opportunities for more flexible interiors, larger column-free spaces and greater efficiency. Shopping centres, hospitals, offices and residential buildings all benefit from these freedoms.

But a larger span does not automatically create a better room.

Technology creates opportunity.

Design determines whether that opportunity is realised.

This distinction is becoming increasingly relevant as construction itself becomes industrialised.

Buildings are gradually shifting from being assembled on site to being manufactured through integrated supply chains. Design decisions increasingly influence fabrication, logistics, sequencing and maintenance simultaneously.

The architect must therefore think beyond geometry.

A wall is no longer simply a wall.

It may also be a manufactured component, a thermal barrier, a structural element, a service zone, a logistics challenge and eventually a recyclable resource.

Technology is making buildings more complex.

It is therefore demanding simpler thinking.

This is perhaps the great paradox of emerging construction technologies.

As our tools become more sophisticated, the importance of first principles only increases.

Good buildings still require clear circulation.

Comfortable proportions.

Natural light.

Appropriate structure.

Legible organisation.

Human scale.

Connection to landscape.

Responsiveness to climate.

These ideas have survived every technological revolution because they arise from human behaviour rather than construction methodology.

People have not fundamentally changed because concrete became stronger.

Children still seek sunlight.

People still gather beneath trees.

Courtyards continue moderating climate.

Well-proportioned rooms remain pleasant regardless of whether their slabs are conventionally reinforced or post-tensioned.

Technology alters the means.

Human experience defines the end.

This has always been architecture’s responsibility.

The danger is not that architects become overly interested in technology.

The danger is allowing technology to dictate architecture rather than inform it.

Every new construction system carries its own temptations. Mivan encourages repetition. Precast encourages modularity. Digital fabrication encourages geometric complexity. Artificial intelligence encourages optimisation.

Each tendency has value.

Each also has limits.

The architect’s role is not to resist technology but to understand where its logic should end.

Architecture has never been a demonstration of construction techniques.

The Gothic cathedral is remembered not because of the flying buttress but because of the space it creates. Louis Kahn’s buildings are admired not for their concrete alone but for the quality of light they contain. The engineering disappears into the experience.

That remains the highest ambition.

Emerging technologies will undoubtedly transform how buildings are conceived, fabricated and assembled. Construction sites will become quieter. Factories will replace labour-intensive processes. Artificial intelligence will coordinate increasingly complex information. Robots may eventually perform tasks once considered impossible to automate.

Yet none of these developments answers the architect’s oldest questions.

Where should people gather?

How should a building meet the ground?

How does it respond to climate?

What gives a place dignity?

How does it age?

Technology cannot answer these questions because they are not technological questions.

They are human ones.

Perhaps that is why every major technological revolution ultimately increases the importance of architectural judgment.

When constraints diminish, choices multiply.

And the more choices technology offers, the greater the need for someone capable of deciding which possibilities deserve to become reality.

Technology changes what buildings can achieve.

Architecture decides what they should become.

## Further Reading

Frampton, K. (1995). *Studies in Tectonic Culture*. MIT Press.

Allen, E., & Iano, J. (2019). *Fundamentals of Building Construction* (7th ed.). Wiley.

Kieran, S., & Timberlake, J. (2004). *Refabricating Architecture*. McGraw-Hill.

Eastman, C., Teicholz, P., Sacks, R., & Liston, K. (2018). *BIM Handbook* (3rd ed.). Wiley.

Brand, S. (1994). *How Buildings Learn: What Happens After They’re Built*. Viking.

Alexander, C. (1979). *The Timeless Way of Building*. Oxford University Press.

Pallasmaa, J. (2012). *The Eyes of the Skin* (3rd ed.). Wiley.

Semper, G. (1860–63). *The Four Elements of Architecture*.
articles.title: Architecture and Emerging Technologies

articles.excerpt: The monsoon should be India's greatest urban celebration. Increasingly, it has become its greatest annual disruption.
articles.body:
Ask someone in northern Europe what rain means, and the answer is often predictable. Grey skies. Cancelled plans. A day to be endured.

Ask the same question in much of India, particularly across the northern plains, and the response is very different.

Rain is relief. A cloudy day is a "good weather today" day! 

The first monsoon clouds interrupt months of relentless heat. Temperatures fall. The air changes. Dust settles. Parks fill. The smell of wet earth "mitti ki khushboo" becomes almost ceremonial. Families move outdoors. Street food tastes better. Chai and pakoras emerge out of every kitchen and many a offices, and on street corners. A cloudy day is not regarded as gloomy; it is often considered a gift.

This relationship with rain is deeply rooted in Indian culture.

Our literature celebrates it. Our music anticipates it. Classical ragas such as "Megh" and "Miyan ki Malhar" evoke it. Festivals coincide with it. Agriculture depends upon it. Across much of the country, the arrival of the southwest monsoon has never simply been a meteorological event.

It has been a season.

Yet something has changed.

For many urban Indians today, the first weather alert no longer brings anticipation.

It brings anxiety.

Will the roads flood? Will traffic come to a standstill? Will the basement fill with water? Will the metro be delayed? Will the electricity fail? (though it must be said: the electricity has become a lot more dependable now, compared to even a few years ago)

The monsoon has not changed nearly as much as our cities have.

India receives approximately 80% of its annual rainfall during the southwest monsoon between June and September. In Delhi, the concentration is even more pronounced, with the overwhelming majority of annual rainfall arriving during these four months. 

This is not an unexpected event.

It is the defining climatic rhythm around which our cities should have evolved.

Instead, many appear surprised by it every year.

The images have become familiar.

Cars submerged beneath flyovers in Delhi. Railway tracks under water in Mumbai. Residential colonies flooded after a few hours of intense rainfall. Overflowing drains. Collapsed roads. Emergency pumps. The prestigious School of Planning and Architecture under 4 feet of water. 

Traffic measured not in kilometres but in hours.

We often describe these as natural disasters.

They are usually failures of urban systems. Rain merely reveals them.

A city does not flood because rain falls. It floods because water has nowhere to go, at least, not at the rate at which it arrives. 

For centuries, Indian settlements understood this intuitively.

Water tanks, stepwells, lakes, ponds, canals and seasonal streams formed integrated hydrological systems. Cities accepted water as a seasonal visitor. Open land absorbed it. Lakes stored it. Floodplains accommodated it.

Urbanisation has steadily dismantled these systems.

Natural drainage channels have been narrowed, covered or encroached upon. Wetlands have become real estate. Open ground has been replaced by asphalt, concrete and paving. Every new impermeable surface increases runoff while reducing infiltration.

The result is paradoxical.

During the monsoon, cities struggle to dispose of water.

A few months later, they struggle to find it.

These are not separate problems.

They are manifestations of the same broken water cycle.

Delhi illustrates this contradiction particularly well.

The city possesses an extensive network of natural drains—or *nallahs*—that historically carried stormwater towards the Yamuna. Many have gradually become concrete channels, informal dumping grounds or disconnected fragments of infrastructure. Their hydraulic function has been compromised precisely when the city has become increasingly dependent upon them. Most of them are unsightly, with sewerage being openly flowing in them. 

The conversation often begins and ends with desilting.

Maintenance certainly matters. Blocked drains reduce capacity. Illegal dumping obstructs flow. Pumping stations require upkeep.

But maintenance alone cannot solve a planning problem.

Every urban decision influences water.

A road.

A parking lot.

A housing colony.

A shopping centre.

A metro station.

A park.

Each changes how rain moves across the landscape.

Yet these decisions are frequently evaluated independently.

Imagine if every significant urban project had to answer one additional question before approval:

**What happens here during a once-in-five-year rainfall event?**

Not after construction.

Before it.

How much additional runoff will this project generate? Where will that water go? Can most of it infiltrate naturally? Can it be stored? Can it recharge groundwater? Can it reduce pressure on downstream drains?

These are not engineering details.

They are urban design questions.

The most resilient cities do not treat stormwater as waste to be removed as quickly as possible.

They treat it as a resource.

Best practices around water has given rise to sponge cities, green infrastructure, bio-swales, rain gardens, permeable pavements and constructed wetlands. The terminology may be contemporary, but the underlying principle is remarkably familiar to India.

Hold water where it falls.

Allow the ground to receive it.

Slow it before accelerating it.

Store it before discarding it.

Traditional Indian settlements understood this long before the vocabulary of sustainable urban drainage was invented.

The challenge is not inventing new ideas.

It is recovering forgotten ones.

This cannot be achieved through isolated demonstration projects.

A rain garden in one neighbourhood cannot compensate for a watershed paved over elsewhere.

Nor can a beautifully designed park solve flooding if surrounding developments continue directing runoff towards it.

The monsoon operates at the scale of the city.

Planning must do the same.

That requires a long-term vision extending beyond electoral cycles and annual maintenance contracts. Drainage networks should be mapped and protected with the same seriousness as road networks.

Natural floodplains should be regarded as infrastructure rather than vacant land awaiting development. Rainwater harvesting should become a measurable performance criterion rather than a regulatory checkbox. Urban landscapes should be evaluated not only for aesthetics but for the volume of water they retain. Development approvals should consider cumulative hydrological impacts rather than isolated site boundaries.

None of these interventions will eliminate flooding overnight, nor should they be expected to.

Cities evolve over decades, so do their water systems.

The objective is not perfection: It is resilience.

There is something deeply unfortunate about teaching an entire generation to associate rain with disruption.

The monsoon deserves better.

It is one of the defining experiences of life in the Indian subcontinent. It replenishes reservoirs, sustains agriculture, cools cities and renews landscapes. It has inspired poets, musicians and painters for centuries.

Our cities should celebrate it rather than defend themselves against it.

That will not happen through larger pumps or deeper drains alone.

It will happen when every decision about roads, housing, landscapes, drainage, transport and public space is understood as part of a single hydrological system.

The question is not whether India can survive the monsoon.

It always has.

The question is whether our cities can once again learn to celebrate it.

## Further Reading

* Gadgil, S. (2003). *The Indian Monsoon and Its Variability*. Annual Review of Earth and Planetary Sciences.
* India Meteorological Department. *Rainfall Climatology of India* and seasonal rainfall reports. 
* McHarg, I. L. (1969). *Design with Nature*. John Wiley & Sons.
* Spirn, A. W. (1984). *The Granite Garden: Urban Nature and Human Design*. Basic Books.
* Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs. *Urban and Regional Development Plans Formulation and Implementation (URDPFI) Guidelines*.
* Kongjian Yu. *Sponge Cities: Theory and Practice* (various lectures and publications).
articles.title: Indian Cities and the Monsoons

notes.title: On walking a site before drawing it
notes.body: The first sketch should happen after your shoes are dusty, not before. The site is potential, the site IS the client. Having a dialogue with the site has to be origin of all architecture: without it, the building is no more than a bicycle shed. And not a very good one at that.

notes.title: Reading
notes.body: The Power Broker, still. It is the best book I know about how a city actually gets made. Man and his Symbols by Carl Jung is another essential reading for understanding signs, symbolism, and all things "conceptual". The other essential readings for architects are Notes on the synthesis of Form by Christopher Alexander, and Ian McHarg's Design with Nature.

notes.title: From a train window
notes.body: The edge of every Indian city is an astonishing laboratory of adaptation. Life exists even where "planning" ends, sometime even more so. Unbound by formality, tied to customs, vernacular emerges from the ground: not always glamorous, not always "local", but always true.

notes.title: Architect Pink FLoyd
notes.body: There is a reason Pink Floyd seems to accompany so many architects late into the night. Their music is less about melody than about constructing space—through silence, rhythm and scale.  Like a well-designed building, a Pink Floyd album unfolds slowly, rewards patience and reveals layers over time. Neither seeks spectacle; both seek resonance.Architects spend their lives designing physical space; Pink Floyd reminds us that space can also be emotional.
